region_of_origin: Singapore (fusion dish combining Thai tom yum broth with Japanese ramen noodles)
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — fusion ramen with spicy Thai tom yum broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tom yum ramen. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for tom yum ramen fusion.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this fusion noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tom yum ramen as served in a Singapore restaurant, approximately 450 g including broth, noodles, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup, with broth and toppings — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for ramen noodle soup and Thai tom yum soup components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy); supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for tom yum broth components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tom yum ramen as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ramen noodle soup entries (FDC ID 172945 — Noodle soup, ramen, any flavor, dry; FDC ID 167898 — Soup, ramen noodle, chicken flavor, dry) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for tom yum soup broth. The composite per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style bowl: approximately 55% broth, 30% noodles, 15% toppings (shrimp, mushrooms, herbs). Energy and macros are weighted averages. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and broth concentration typical of tom yum preparations.

gi_source.title: Noodle soup GI estimate based on wheat noodles in broth — analogous to 'Instant noodles, boiled' and 'Noodle soup' entries in the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tom yum ramen. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles in soup (GI ~47–55 range for boiled wheat noodles in broth). The spicy tom yum broth, protein content from shrimp, and fat content from coconut-free broth are expected to moderately lower the glycemic response relative to plain noodles. GI 52 is a conservative mid-range estimate within the low GI band. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 42.75) / 100 = 22.2, rounded to 22.3. Serving carbohydrate = 9.5 g per 100g × 450g serving = 42.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from fish sauce, broth, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g bowl of tom yum ramen provides approximately 2,160 mg of sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit portion size, and avoid drinking the remaining soup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat ramen noodles, partially offset by protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 22 per bowl, tom yum ramen poses a moderate postprandial glucose risk; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, choose smaller noodle portions, and pair with additional protein to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth and protein toppings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 2,160 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (approximately 2.7 g per bowl) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, request lower-sodium preparations, and ensure overall daily sodium intake remains within clinical targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Soup Ramen is a fusion noodle dish that combines the bold, aromatic flavours of Thai tom yum broth with Japanese-style ramen wheat noodles. The broth is characteristically sour, spicy, and fragrant, built from a long-simmered base of lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chilli, mushrooms, and fish sauce, finished with lime juice for brightness. The ramen noodles — springy, chewy wheat noodles — absorb the complex broth and provide the primary carbohydrate base of the dish. Toppings typically include shrimp or other protein, mushrooms, and fresh herbs. In Singapore, this dish is served at fusion and modern Asian restaurants as a creative crossover between Thai and Japanese culinary traditions. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 324 kcal, 18.9 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and 42.75 g carbohydrate. The dish is notably high in sodium (approximately 2,160 mg per bowl) due to fish sauce and seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI), but the glycemic load per bowl is moderate at approximately 22, relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with compliant ingredients. It is not suitable for those with gluten intolerance due to wheat noodles, and shellfish allergens are present when shrimp is included.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tom yum ramen as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ramen noodle soup entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA tom yum broth component data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle soup entries. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but direct measurement data for this specific fusion dish is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers tom yum ramen as a unified dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if direct laboratory analysis or an approved-source composite entry becomes available. 2. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on restaurant preparation; the estimate of 480 mg per 100g (2,160 mg per bowl) reflects a typical restaurant-style tom yum broth with fish sauce. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central ramen soup entries used for this estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central ramen soup entries used for this estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data used for this estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a broth-based noodle soup with no hydrogenated fat ingredients. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). 8. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Tom Yummy Soup RAMEN' to the canonical English form 'Tom Yum Soup Ramen'. 9. Halal status is conditional on restaurant preparation using halal-certified fish sauce and protein ingredients. 10. This dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan (field survey data source record GD003073).
